title: The 125-Year-Old Wool Jacket Brands Still Can’t Beat.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Hey, why did milkmen and delivery boys wear this specific vest?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Initially, everything made in this fabric, like this vest or that jacket, those were designed specifically for outdoorsmen or lumberjacks or something like that in the cold.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: However, this fabric, the formula of the jackets and the vests were so special that all of a sudden everybody was dying to get anything they could in this fabric.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So your local lumberjack, hunters, fishermen, all wearing it, your delivery boys were wearing it, but also people in Antarctica were wearing it on first ever US expeditions and crazy things.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Mainly because of this fabric, like I said.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Sup everybody, it's Michael, don't worry, we are going to the woods. Taylor just asked me if I could get cream for her from the grocery store, and I said yeah, of course, maybe I could maybe you could watch me throw it in the air for about 20 minutes for a video.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Today's video will cover Brown's beach cloth garments, introduced by William W. Brown in 1901.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: William Brown's fabric in the 1900s was basically considered like a space age fabric. It was mind blowing. People were writing him letters begging him to never change the configuration of it because it was perfect.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But what's interesting at the same time is he kind of had to hide the secret of his fabric, because his fabric was trash, shoddy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Mr. Brown's shoddy trash fabric, though, had one major trick up its sleeve. It was virtually unbeatable in situations like this. More comfortable, more more more breathable, wind resistant, water resistant, and really elastic. That's what he kept saying. Elastic.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Brown's beach cloth fabric, developed in Connecticut and then later made in Worcester, Massachusetts, very close to where I'm from, very proud of that fact. We're breaking this video up into three distinct sections.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Number one, why do I keep calling Brown's beach cloth shoddy trash?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Point number two, this is a very weird fabric and most of the time people get it including me before I did this video, people get it wrong when they're talking about what this is.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And finally, number three, besides the fabric being really cool and it being shoddy trash, there are a bunch of other features that made the jacket so fit, well the vest specifically, so famous for the time. Why did people in the 1920s and 1960s up until then like them so much? And then you know what came in the 1960s and destroyed everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But either way, that's the blueprint for today's video. Let's go.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Oh also, at the end of this trail is not that far in the woods, but there's a waterfall. Gonna see what happens when it's 10 degrees outside. Haven't seen it yet in the winter, so we can test the water resistance of this jacket there too.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Theoretically. I'm wearing all wool besides all this cotton, so I shouldn't die.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: William Brown and Brown's beach cloth had a secret, and it wasn't really a secret, but he didn't want to advertise it at all because it was hated in America, had a terrible reputation, and that was shoddy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So the thing is, William Brown used shoddy and basically...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: it's the only way where it didn't make a garment worse, but you're probably thinking what the heck is shoddy, why are we doing this part before that part? But that would be more interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Shoddy was just what they called recycled fabric way back when in the 1800s. First started off in England, and that's where it got the name shoddy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And the process of making recycled clothing really hasn't changed since the 1800s. It's the same thing. You're taking a woolen cloth, usually a rag in this case, you're grinding it up into shorter fibers, you're making it into a yarn, and then you re-weave that yarn.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So for a while, shoddy kind of has the same reputation that polyester has now, where people are like, no, I want 100% cotton or 100% wool. You're just putting polyester in to make it cheaper. Some people didn't like that, some people didn't care, other people understood why they were doing that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But then the Civil War happened and (sound effect). The Civil War had a lot of people enlisting very, very, very fast, and the US government was like, hey clothing brands, we need uniforms very fast. So they basically made junk uniforms that didn't fit, they didn't have buttonholes, or they were missing buttons, they fell apart in the rain because they were glued together scraps of fabric, absolute junk. And what did soldiers call that? Shoddy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So all of a sudden a lot of laws get passed. The machines that make shoddy in England are nicknamed and were always nicknamed devils, and what they made was called devil's dust. Shoddy was not doing so well.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And then finally, that leads to our man William Brown, whose fabric was just getting extremely popular. So he was kind of not trying to advertise that it was a repurposed wool, but also repurposed wool at that time had a certain set of standards that made it a little higher quality. But it didn't matter the quality because he was doing something clever.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But first, a quick ad. Thank you for the support.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This video is sponsored by Runna, a personalized automated running coaching app.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It offers training plans from your first 5k all the way to ultramarathons. I'm personally going to run an ultramarathon. Just kidding, obviously, but I am running my first ever half marathon with Runna and I'm very excited about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Last sponsor we ran through the whole setup process, through the whole list of runs for me to do on certain days that I could add to my calendar. There's a nice little lady that speaks in my ear that tells me to go faster or slower. Runna just makes it very motivating to run in general. Although today I am not the most motivated because it's negative 2 degrees Fahrenheit outside and that's always a not a fun run.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But we must... Oh, I just want to show you the sunset... sunrise. This is the race I'm going to run, feel free to join me if you can catch me.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And then this is my progress so far. Hmm, not the best sunrise, but that's okay. The sun is probably frozen because it's negative 2.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So if you want to make running a sustainable habit, get fitter, or start training for a specific race like the half marathon that I'm doing, check out Runna in the description. Thank you very much Runna. It's very cold, but I feel very motivated.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Beach cloth is actually one of the most nerdy fabrics that you can possibly have because it's a multi-fibered fabric where using cotton and wool, not just wool, and it's a knit, it is not a woven, but it's a specific type of knit, not like what you see on a t-shirt or a sweatshirt.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: More so like what you see on a bag of oranges at the grocery store or a soccer net.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: There's two main ways of making fabric. There is weaving and there is knitting.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: is knitting. When we're thinking of woven goods, think of like my pants, denim jeans, canvas, Oxford cloth, button downs and stuff like that. It's a firm fabric that doesn't stretch, holds its structure, really good against abrasion, tough workwear. That's a grid, so we have warp yarns and then we have weft yarns that pass through.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Then we have knitting, which is all fundamentally loops. That's it. Weft knitting is what we're most used to on t-shirts and sweaters. The ribbing of this mitten is very stretchy because you have a bunch of loops that can stretch out when you pull on them. It's very comfortable, oftentimes not as dense as a woven fabric to get the same thickness. So it's airy, a little warmer for weight ratio. But it doesn't hold its shape over long periods of time. It could bag and sag out, and if you snag it, it could run and kind of unravel.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So Brown's Beach Cloth uses a form of knitting called warp knitting. Weft knitting is a bunch of loops, one main yarn, and it interlocks those loops one at a time. Warp knitting on the other hand is a bunch of yarns and a bunch of loops happening at the same time. And it has kind of a blend between knitting and weaving. It is just knitting, but it has good qualities of both.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The first thing that it doesn't do is unravel or ladder if there is a hole in it. So if you think of a soccer net, if you kicked a ball in it and there was a hole in that net, it doesn't go pfft and just open up.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: It's also more structurally stable, so if you have bullets or heavy tools or something down here, it's not going to stretch out and bag out and lose shape and kind of sag down all day.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But at the same time, it is still a knit, and it is still based on loops, so you have a lot more flexibility, a lot more comfort, and the loft is higher because you can lay in a fleece, which is what's happening here.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Which means, which is the coolest part, it's just about as warm as a big woven wool jacket. I just spent about 20 minutes standing right there stationary eating my filming snack. Felt pretty comfortable, a little cold, hands are cold. But I broke my microphone again yesterday. I got a little too excited and tried to talk about the buttons and I ripped the microphone from the cord. All of a sudden a branch gets caught here and then I just kept going. Absolutely no idea I was talking to no one for about 45 minutes doing my thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And today I ripped the clip that goes on here off, so we have to pinch it like this. So my hands are cold. So if you were in the 1920s you'd already be impressed that it gets close to a woven wool coat, but it's also about 1/3 of the weight because of this lofted fleece.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But the ingenuity of Brown's beach cloth doesn't end there. It's all about the fibers in the specific wool-cotton blend they're using that makes it so brilliant and makes it functional. Otherwise it wouldn't work if they did just cotton or just wool.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Oh, well. That's a waterfall. That's... I don't know why I was expecting to see like Niagara Falls or something here. I've seen it a few months ago. It's pretty not spectacular then either. Hopefully you weren't like 'well, I don't really care about Brown's Beach Cloth but I'd love to see that waterfall. He really sold me on it.' Whatever, use AI, make an AI waterfall. Look at that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So we can break Brown's Beach Cloth into two distinct areas. The front or the outside and then the back or the inside. The fluffy and the not fluffy. The outside zigzag salt and pepper part of the fabric is two ply.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: yarn cotton so two yarns spun around each other and then knit into loops and everything like that. That is very useful.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And cotton is and always probably has been cheaper than wool, so that reduces the cost right away.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But also, wool is strong, but it's very elastic and not as strong as cotton, especially when it's wet.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So a really big woven wool jacket is fine, but when you're playing with a knit, you probably want some more abrasion resistance, strength, and stuff like that, which we talked about in the Merino t-shirt video.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So there you could see how much stronger cotton is.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You could also see in that graph that it's not as stretchy as wool, which is important when you want your knit piece to hold its shape a little bit better.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But on the inside, that's where it gets even more brilliant and we get the recycling devil machine things from the early 1800s in full bore.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This, my friends, is our shoddy trash hated during the Civil War part of the fabric.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This is recycled wool, or theoretically it was. I don't know if the modern versions are.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Recycled mechanically ground-up fabrics are weaker because we're taking a wool fiber or cotton fiber that's originally say this long and then we're making it that long.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But fleece is a bursted yarn. So you basically get a machine, you rip up all of the loops and everything that go into making a fleece and then you screw it all up and destroy the fibers so they're fluffy and lofted.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Those long fiber lengths don't matter because you're going to destroy the yarns anyways as part of the use of the garment, so you get functionally no loss in durability and luster and anything like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You're doing it anyways. Why spend more money to do the same thing?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So now imagine saying to someone in the 1900s, 'Hey, I know you like your big heavy woven jacket, but what about this one? It's cheaper, lighter, more breathable, possibly warmer, super durable, and it looks great.'

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: They're probably going to be sold pretty darn fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But it also didn't end there when they were selling this to the customer because Brown is using a knit fabric, so he has to make certain compromises on the design of his jacket.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But what's crazy is those compromises that he had to make only made people like the jacket more.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So now we're going to go over the compromises.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: The first compromise that Brown had to make, which by the way, I am the one saying compromise, I don't know if he actually thought of these as compromises...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: ...but the distinct binding that we have all around the pockets, all on the placket, all on the bottom of the hem.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This woven binding adds durability, some structure so it doesn't bag out, and reduces overall bulk, which I would assume was their main focus.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And everything is rounded because of the binding. They're trying to have it flow better, so it doesn't have to keep going to points like it does right here.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And then we have these...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: ...these old microphone destroyers, also known as snap buttons.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Snap button technology debuted in the late 1800s, got popular by the 1900s.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Probably really important for Brown's Beach Cloth because a buttonhole might sag out over time and the button would just fall out since again, this is a knit.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: This, on the other hand, is pressed and they have some reinforcement tape on the back, which I guess they could have done for buttons.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: So let's also assume that this was a choice they had for convenience.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And finally, the greatest feature that Mr. Brown added was actually a subtraction.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: I just did not have a good transition to go to this, but without...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Further ado, the vest. The most important product that Mr. Brown ever released. This is a capital version, so it's a little more artsy-fartsy than the original. But either way, the grand finale is the vest.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: What's so important about the vest? Well, first off, vests are just awesome. Obviously, vests aren't really that popular anymore in 2026, but now I'm totally going to pick one up because they're so convenient. You get so much more movement in your arms, you can layer better, it's awesome.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: Probably going to grab one from, I forget the first part of the name, but Baxter local to me, really cool guy, makes stuff for Filson. But besides being convenient and way more popular back then, they're also a great way to try a new piece of technology without having to redo your entire kit.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: You didn't have to buy a new jacket, you could put this on under your kit and it improved it because of all the qualities that we talked about before. All of a sudden you take off your woven vest, put on this knit one, you're lighter, more breathable, more comfortable, everything like that. Bing bang boom, you want the whole Brown fleet because he made booties, hats, and everything like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: And I could really see why. It's effectively a test drive to a revolutionary new fabric that changed the world forever. Synthetic fibers like polyester, nylon, stretch, and everything like that kind of railroaded Brown's Beach Cloth and killed the company.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: But I think if that never happened, most people, most professions would gravitate to Brown's Beach Cloth or a warp-knit or even a weft-knit fabric for their daily workwear. Then now in 2026, that's kind of really going crazy for athleisure, for sporting, for everything like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: People are mostly wearing knits and I have a theory that in five to ten years, even pretty dangerous professions and stuff like that will be heavily based on knit clothing and woven clothing is going to become specialty heritage stuff almost completely. And Mr. William W. Brown was kind of the first guy to be like, this is it, this is the future.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Kristy
full_transcript.text: That is about it, I'll see you in the next video. I'm sampling Brown's Beach Cloth for a super secret clothing project and the Mammoth is releasing very soon along with other denim. Tell you about that in a video coming soon, but it's all coming. Sorry about that.

highlights: Brown's Beach Cloth, introduced in 1901, utilized a unique "warp knit" construction that combined the structural stability of woven fabric with the flexibility and breathability of a knit.

The fabric’s secret was its use of "shoddy," or recycled wool, which was mechanically ground down to create a lofted fleece that provided significant warmth while being much lighter than traditional wool jackets.
Key design features like woven binding and snap buttons were essential innovations used to give the elastic knit fabric the durability and shape retention required for heavy-duty workwear.
Though eventually replaced by synthetic materials in the 1960s, the fabric was once so highly regarded for its performance that it was used by both everyday workers and Antarctic explorers.
summary:
This transcript features Michael Kristy discussing the history and technical innovation of Brown’s Beach Cloth, a legendary outdoor fabric introduced by William W. Brown in 1901. Initially popular among lumberjacks, delivery boys, and even Antarctic explorers, the material was revolutionary for its time. Kristy highlights a central irony: while the fabric was considered "space age," it was actually made from "shoddy"—a term for recycled wool fibers that carried a negative reputation due to low-quality military uniforms during the American Civil War. Brown successfully repurposed this material by focusing on a specific warp-knit construction, which offered the structural stability of a woven fabric with the comfort and elasticity of a knit.

The fabric’s functionality stems from a strategic blend of a rugged cotton exterior and a lofted, recycled wool fleece interior. This combination made the garments significantly lighter than traditional woven wool coats while remaining wind-resistant and warm. Because the material was a knit, Brown had to incorporate unique design compromises that became iconic features, such as woven binding on the edges to prevent sagging and snap buttons to ensure durability. Though the company eventually fell victim to the rise of synthetic fibers in the 1960s, Kristy argues that Brown was a visionary whose work paved the way for modern performance knits and the eventual shift toward knit-based professional workwear.
description:
Thanks, Runna! Train for your next race or get in shape with 2 weeks free! 
https://web.runna.com/redeem?code=IRONSNAIL

The greatest wool coat? Perhaps for you. Maybe not. You may like Filson, Weather Wool, THE IRON SNAIL, or some other brand. However -- you'd be hard-pressed to find a lighter, more unique, and crazier wool coat than Brown's Beach Cloth!! Strap in as we dive into one of the weirdest wool coats there has been! 

Jackets Worn In The Video: 

Anatomica's Beach Jacket: 
https://anatomica-nagoya.com/ja-us/products/beach-jacket-salt-pepper?_pos=3&_sid=b2a84c7ae&_ss=r

Schott x Brown's Beach Cloth Jacket (discontinued but on eBay)

Kapital's Brown Beach Cloth Vest (discontinued but also on eBay)

Fullcount's Brown's Beach Cloth (they own the brand now, but their 38 was too small for me):

The Iron Snail is a men’s fashion vlog starring a young man named Michael and featuring a snail no bigger than a quarter. The two are set on taking over the world of fashion by creating a clothing line to end all clothing lines. Until then, we’re here to tell you EVERYTHING you need to know about the best clothing out there, from the highest quality raw denim jeans to the warmest jackets to the sturdiest boots…the Iron Snail has got you covered. (written by Taylor, though Michael is sometimes known to talk about himself in the third person… xoxo)
topics: The history and development of Brown's Beach Cloth fabric
The manufacturing process and historical reputation of 'shoddy' recycled wool
Technical differences between weaving, weft knitting, and warp knitting
Design features and functional advantages of vintage workwear vests
The evolution of garment fabrics from traditional wovens to modern knits
